map.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing   •   1 day   •   LO IOC-1.F
map.intro: Everything for this topic lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Day 1 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, share-out cues, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with the QOTD warm-up, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Day 1 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Portfolio Rights Audit and Exercise 2: Rights Route Advisor (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Who Owns an Idea? (3-part: Opening, Mid-lesson, Closing — all inside the single teach day)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 7 questions on LO IOC-1.F, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the instruction you project and teach from (one teach day: intellectual property and plagiarism, the three legal routes plus the always-cite rule, then computing's wider legal and ethical concerns).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Scheduling: Topic 5.5 is a 1-day topic. The quiz runs as the opener of the NEXT class (spaced retrieval), so the topic never silently consumes a second day. Topic 5.6 (Safe Computing) begins immediately after the quiz.

exercise1.title: Portfolio Rights Audit
exercise1.intro: A classmate is about to publish a personal portfolio website and has asked you to audit it first. Five of the site's assets were borrowed from somewhere else. For each asset you will read where it came from and how it is used, then rule: which legal route covers it (Creative Commons, open source, open access), or is it a violation — and what fix and citation would make it right? Part A works the audit log; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objective IOC-1.F.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Audit Log: Five Borrowed Assets Awaiting a Ruling
exercise1.narrative: The classmate exported a list of every asset on the site that they did not create themselves, with notes on where each one came from. Your job is to audit them before the site goes live. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: name the route or the violation, prescribe the fix, then write the credit line the asset still needs.

rows: Hero background photo
Saved from a photography blog found through an image search
No license mentioned; the photographer was never asked
Full-screen background on the home page, no credit anywhere
Page-transition code
Copied from a code-hosting site; the project's license says free to use, modify, and redistribute
Open-source license, clearly posted by the project
Pasted into the site's code with the classmate's name in the file header
Background music loop
Downloaded from a music archive; the track page shows a CC BY tag
Creative Commons license posted by the musician
Plays on the projects page; a caption names the musician and links the source
Statistics in an essay page
Numbers quoted from a research study that is free to read online, no restrictions
Open-access study; free of access restrictions
Presented inside the essay as the classmate's own analysis, no source named
Game-project code excerpt
A friend's Create PT program, shared after a verbal 'sure, go ahead'
Spoken permission only; nothing in writing
Displayed in the 'My code' section with no acknowledgement of the friend

exercise1.scenarioNote: Assets adapted from the Day 1 bell ringer and the route-or-violation worked table; terminology and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which one asset looks legal at first glance but is actually the most serious problem, and what single word in its log row gives it away?

partA.q: Asset 1 (hero photo): the classmate argues 'it came up in an image search on a public blog, so it's fair game.' Rule on the argument using EK IOC-1.F.1 and F.2, then prescribe the complete fix — both the permission side and the credit side.
partA.answer: The argument fails. The photo is the photographer's intellectual property — material created on a computer belongs to its creator or an organization (EK IOC-1.F.1) — and appearing in a search result changes nothing. In fact, the ease of finding and copying it is exactly why the CED flags ownership, value, and use as concerns (EK IOC-1.F.2). Fix: (1) permission — ask the photographer for a yes in writing, or replace the photo with one carrying a Creative Commons tag that permits reuse; (2) credit — a caption or site-credits entry naming the photographer and linking the original page (EK IOC-1.F.6). Full credit requires BOTH halves; 'just add a credit line' without permission is still a violation.

partA.q: Assets 2 and 3: name the legal route covering each one. Then rule which of the two is fully in the clear as-is, and state exactly what the other is missing and how to fix it (include the citation format for borrowed code).
partA.answer: Asset 2 is OPEN SOURCE — the posted license makes the program freely available to use, modify, and redistribute (EK IOC-1.F.5). Asset 3 is CREATIVE COMMONS — a public copyright license that lets the musician's otherwise copyrighted track be freely distributed and built upon (EK IOC-1.F.5). Asset 3 is fully in the clear: route present, and the caption naming the musician with a link satisfies the always-cite rule (EK IOC-1.F.6). Asset 2 has the route but fails the credit: the classmate's own name in the file header presents borrowed code as their own. Fix: an acknowledgement in the program documentation naming the project (origin) and its authors, with the source URL — the same acknowledgement format required for borrowed code segments since Topic 1.3 (LO CRD-2.H).

partA.q: Asset 4 (open-access statistics): the study really is free of access restrictions — yet this asset is still a violation. Explain precisely what open access did and did not make legal here, name the violation, and write the fix.
partA.answer: Open access made the ACCESS legal: the study is online research output free of any and all restrictions on access and free of many restrictions on use (EK IOC-1.F.5), so reading and quoting it is permitted. What no route ever grants is presenting someone else's work as your own: the essay uses the researchers' findings without naming them and passes them off as the classmate's analysis — using material created by someone else and presenting it as one's own is plagiarism and may have legal consequences (EK IOC-1.F.4). Fix: cite the study — authors, title, and where it can be found — at the point of use (EK IOC-1.F.6), and reword the essay so the findings are attributed rather than claimed. The lesson: a legal route answers permission; it never waives credit.

partA.q: Asset 5 (the friend's Create PT code): permission WAS given, so what is still wrong? Rule on the asset, write the acknowledgement line the 'My code' section needs, and name two measures the friend could take to safeguard their own program in the future.
partA.answer: Permission solves the legal-use half, but the display is labeled 'My code' with no acknowledgement — presenting the friend's work as the classmate's own, which is the second half of plagiarism's definition (EK IOC-1.F.4). Fix the label and add the acknowledgement: 'Collision routine adapted from [friend's name]'s Create PT program, used with permission' — an acknowledgement should include the origin or the original author's name. Safeguarding measures the friend could take (EK IOC-1.F.3): put the permission in writing and scope it (where, how long, with what credit); attach an explicit license to shared code instead of a verbal okay; keep dated copies proving authorship. Bonus insight: written terms protect BOTH students — the CED expects measures to be taken to safeguard intellectual property.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: The CED says Creative Commons, open source, and open access 'have enabled broad access to digital information.' Using two assets from the audit log, explain what this portfolio could and could not legally contain in a world where none of the three routes existed.
partB.answer: Without the three routes, every reuse would require individually negotiated permission from each rights holder. Asset 3's music loop could not exist unless the classmate personally contacted the musician and struck a deal — the CC BY tag replaces that negotiation with a standing public license (EK IOC-1.F.5). Asset 2's page-transition code could not legally be copied or modified at all, since standard copyright grants no such rights — the open-source license is what makes redistribution and modification lawful. That is EK IOC-1.F.7's point: the three routes turned reuse from a case-by-case legal negotiation into an ecosystem, enabling the broad access to digital information that modern sites, apps, and research depend on.

partB.q: The finished portfolio is itself intellectual property. Name two realistic measures the classmate should take to safeguard it once published, and explain — using EK IOC-1.F.2 — why publishing online makes those measures worth taking.
partB.answer: The portfolio is the classmate's intellectual property the moment it is created (EK IOC-1.F.1), and measures should be taken to safeguard it (EK IOC-1.F.3). Realistic measures: attach an explicit license (for example, a Creative Commons tag choosing exactly what others may do); add a visible copyright/credit notice with the year and name; watermark showcase images; keep dated project files that prove authorship. Publishing makes this worth doing precisely due to EK IOC-1.F.2: once digitized and online, the work can be copied and redistributed in one click, so ownership, value, and use are immediately at risk — the same ease of access the classmate exploited in Asset 1 now points back at their own work.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: The music archive offers the same loop under three tags: CC BY, CC BY-ND, and CC BY-NC-SA. The classmate wants to shorten the loop, layer a beat under it, and maybe sell the remix later. Which tag(s) permit that plan, which forbid it, and what does every one of the three tags require no matter what?
partB.answer: CC BY permits the whole plan: remixing (shortening, layering) and even commercial sale are allowed as long as the musician is credited. CC BY-ND forbids it at the first step — ND (no derivatives) bars publishing altered versions, so the shortened, layered loop cannot legally be shared at all. CC BY-NC-SA permits the remix but blocks the business: NC rules out selling, and SA forces any published remix to carry the same BY-NC-SA license. What all three require regardless: BY — credit the musician — which mirrors the CED's unconditional rule that material created by someone else should always be cited (EK IOC-1.F.6). Tag-reading is beyond the exam; the always-cite rule is not.

exercise2.title: Rights Route Advisor
exercise2.subtitle: 5 consults - rule and prescribe
exercise2.intro: You are the on-call advisor for five clients, each stuck on a legal-or-ethical computing question. For each consult, name the legal route (Creative Commons, open source, open access) or the violation or concern — using CED vocabulary — then prescribe the fix and the credit still owed. Advanced students also layer a second concern (harm, bias, monitoring, or the digital divide) onto the same consult. Vague 'just don't steal' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): the route, violation, or concern named with CED vocabulary + the fix and required credit, for all 5 consults. Enrichment (optional): also layer a second concern from EK IOC-1.F.8–F.11 for a more complete ruling.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every consult
method: Anchor your gut ruling before analyzing. Writing your own answer first keeps a convincing wrong option from swaying you.
Step 2 - Name the route, violation, or concern
Match the facts to the CED category: Creative Commons, open source, or open access — or plagiarism, harm, bias, monitoring, or the digital divide. The name decides the ruling.
Step 3 - Prescribe the fix + the credit

State what makes the use legitimate (permission, the right license, a different design) AND the citation still owed — material created by someone else should always be cited. A ruling without its credit line is incomplete.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (layering the wider concerns): strong rulings notice what else the consult touches. A legal-use question can hide a monitoring concern (who else sees the data?), a bias concern (who does the algorithm mistreat?), or a divide concern (who gets locked out?). The CED's example list — streaming/download software, biased algorithms, continuously monitoring devices (EK IOC-1.F.11) — plus the digital divide (EK IOC-1.F.10) are the layers to check.
fields: Ruling (route, violation, or concern — named with CED vocabulary):
Fix + required credit (what makes it legitimate):
Enrichment — second concern to layer (harm, bias, monitoring, divide):

scenarios.text: A student drops a CC-tagged illustration into a history presentation. The Creative Commons tag clearly allows reuse in schoolwork, so the student figures the legal side is handled and lists no sources at all. Advise the student.

key.signs: The route is real: the illustration carries a Creative Commons tag — a public copyright license that lets the otherwise copyrighted work be freely shared, used, and built upon (EK IOC-1.F.5). So the USE is legal. The problem is the empty source list: material created by someone other than you should always be cited (EK IOC-1.F.6), and a license never waives that rule.
key.mechanism: Fix: keep the illustration — no permission problem exists — and add the credit: the artist's name, the work's title or page, and the license (for example, 'Illustration by [artist], CC-licensed'). The one-sentence rule for the student: a license answers 'may I use it?'; a citation answers 'who made it?'; the exam and the classroom require both. Skipping credit presents the work as the student's own, which is half of plagiarism's definition (EK IOC-1.F.4).
key.subtype: Layer: none of the heavier concerns apply here — which is itself worth noticing. This consult is the baseline case: route present, credit missing, five-second fix. Strong advisors keep it separated from the violation cases so 'CC-tagged' never gets lumped in with 'taken.'

scenarios.text: A gaming channel re-uploads another creator's highlight video, found 'free online,' with new thumbnails and a monetized ad slot. The channel owner argues: the original was posted publicly, views are free, and re-uploading is 'how the Internet works.' Advise the channel.

key.signs: Violation. The video is the original creator's intellectual property (EK IOC-1.F.1) — public posting is a viewing convenience, not an ownership transfer. Re-uploading it as channel content, monetized, without permission and presented as the channel's own is plagiarism and may have legal consequences (EK IOC-1.F.4): takedowns, demonetization, lawsuits. The 'free online' argument is exactly the ownership/value/use concern EK IOC-1.F.2 predicts from easy digital distribution.
key.mechanism: Fix: obtain permission — a written agreement with the creator, or use only footage the creator released under a license permitting reuse (for example, a Creative Commons tag allowing commercial derivatives). Then credit prominently: the creator's name and original link (EK IOC-1.F.6). If no route can be secured, the upload must come down; there is no citation format that legalizes an unpermitted commercial re-upload.
key.subtype: Layer: this consult sits on the CED's first example concern — software that allows digital media downloads and streaming (EK IOC-1.F.11). The same platform tools that let creators reach millions make wholesale re-uploading trivial, which is why platforms build detection systems; the legal concern and the enabling technology arrived together.

scenarios.text: A robotics team finds another team's autopilot code published under an open-source license. They want to modify it heavily for their own robot and enter regional competition with it. One member objects: 'Changing someone's code without asking is stealing.' Advise the team.

key.signs: The route is OPEN SOURCE: programs made freely available that may be redistributed and modified (EK IOC-1.F.5). The objecting member has it backwards — modification without asking is not stealing here; it is exactly what the license pre-authorizes. No additional permission is required, and this openness is part of why the three routes have enabled broad access to digital information (EK IOC-1.F.7).
key.mechanism: Fix: proceed, but complete the credit half: an acknowledgement in the program documentation naming the origin project and its authors, plus a note on what was modified — the same acknowledgement rule that governs borrowed code segments in class and on the Create PT (EK IOC-1.F.6). Competition judges should be able to see at a glance which code is inherited and which is the team's own work. Check the license's own terms too: some open-source licenses ask that redistributed versions keep the license text attached.
key.subtype: Layer: an ethics question survives the legal green light — competitive credit. Even with a legal route and a proper citation, the team should ask whether the event's rules treat heavily-inherited code as the team's engineering. Legal (may we?) and ethical (should we, and how do we present it?) are separate checks; this topic's title contains both words on purpose.

scenarios.text: A startup builds a paid study-summary app. Its pipeline ingests two feeds: (a) open-access research papers, and (b) articles scraped from a subscription journal by borrowing a university's login. Summaries appear in the app with no sources listed 'to keep the interface clean.' Advise the startup before launch.

key.signs: Split ruling — one consult, three findings. Feed (a) rides a real route: open access is online research output free of any and all restrictions on access and free of many restrictions on use (EK IOC-1.F.5), so ingesting and summarizing it can be legitimate. Feed (b) is a violation: the paywalled articles are the publisher's and authors' intellectual property (EK IOC-1.F.1), and a borrowed login is access without permission — the ownership, value, and use concern of EK IOC-1.F.2 in its rawest commercial form. Finding three: 'no sources listed' fails the always-cite rule (EK IOC-1.F.6) across BOTH feeds and presents the researchers' findings as app content (EK IOC-1.F.4).
key.mechanism: Fix: drop or re-source feed (b) — license the subscription content properly or exclude it; a paid product cannot stand on scraped paywalled work. Keep feed (a) and add attribution to every summary: authors, paper title, and a link — a clean interface is a design constraint, not a legal route. The startup should also verify each open-access paper's remaining use restrictions: 'free of MANY restrictions' is not 'free of all,' and some open-access licenses restrict commercial reuse.
key.subtype: Layer: the open-access movement exists to democratize research (EK IOC-1.F.7) — and note the divide angle (EK IOC-1.F.10): a paid app that summarizes free-to-read science re-erects a small paywall in front of it. Legal once feed (b) is fixed, but the ethics of charging for access to freely licensed knowledge is exactly the kind of concern LO IOC-1.F asks students to articulate.

scenarios.text: A district accepts free 'engagement analytics' software for school laptops. It runs continuously — logging keystrokes, webcam attention checks, and app usage at home and school — and flags 'low-engagement students' with an algorithm trained on last year's top-performing classes, which skewed wealthy. Families without district laptops must install it on personal devices to submit homework. Advise the district.

key.signs: No copyright question at all — and that is the test. This consult is LO IOC-1.F's wider half: (1) a computing device/software that collects and analyzes data by continuously monitoring activities — the CED's third example concern (EK IOC-1.F.11), sharpened by webcam checks reaching into homes; (2) an algorithm that includes bias — trained on a wealthy cohort, its 'low-engagement' flags will systematically mislabel students unlike that cohort (EK IOC-1.F.11); (3) using computing in ways that can harm individuals or groups raises legal and ethical concerns (EK IOC-1.F.8) — mislabeled students face real consequences; and (4) the personal-device requirement leans on the digital divide, an ethical concern in its own right (EK IOC-1.F.10).
key.mechanism: Fix: 'free' is not the price — the district should renegotiate or decline. Minimum conditions: strip always-on and at-home monitoring down to disclosed, in-class, opt-in telemetry; demand the vendor document and correct the training-data skew before any flag touches a student record; guarantee a no-install path for homework submission so no family's access depends on hosting surveillance software; and put human review between the algorithm and any intervention. Each condition maps to the concern it neutralizes: monitoring, bias, harm, divide.
key.subtype: Layer: the consult shows why the topic pairs LEGAL with ETHICAL — much of this package might be lawful under signed terms of service, and wrong anyway. Strong advisors separate the two explicitly: what the law permits (contracts, consent forms) versus what the district should do to students it exists to serve. On the exam, that separation is the skill EK IOC-1.F.8–F.11 items reward.

discussion.title: Who Owns an Idea?
discussion.minutes: ~20 min total - 3 parts - Opening + Mid-lesson + Closing (all Day 1)
discussion.overview: Every day you consume — and produce — a stream of remixed material: memes built on unlicensed screenshots, edits of other people's songs, code copied from strangers on the Internet. Somebody made every ingredient, and the law says each one has an owner. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will decide whether remix culture is theft or tribute, write the one rule that separates legal reuse from plagiarism, and audit a class showcase site the way a rights lawyer would — including the concerns that have nothing to do with copyright at all.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Memes are remixes of images and clips their makers almost never licensed. Is meme culture mass plagiarism, harmless tribute, or an unwritten license everyone silently agrees to? Where does your group draw the line — and does the line move if the meme makes money?

Question 2. You post an original drawing, beat, or program online tonight. What do you WANT strangers to be allowed to do with it — share it, remix it, sell it, nothing? Now the hard part: how would a stranger ever know your answer? Invent the mechanism.
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the route-or-violation worked table
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The two-question rule
items.text: Every case in today's table was settled by the same two checks. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, write the two-question rule that separates legal, honest reuse from plagiarism — and name the one duty that no license ever removes.

items.label: Task B - Should everything be open?
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: open source turned free sharing into an engine that built much of the modern Internet. Should music and movies work the same way — everything remixable by default, with credit? Record your group's position and the strongest cost of the side you chose (what creators lose, or what culture loses).

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after Common AP Traps
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The showcase-site audit
items.text: Your class is publishing a public showcase site: student project write-ups, borrowed background music, a code library from the Internet, quoted statistics from research papers, and a built-in analytics script that logs every visitor's clicks. Write the group's rights policy: which assets need which legal route, what the site's credit lines must contain, what safeguards protect the STUDENTS' own work — and name one concern the site raises that has nothing to do with copyright (think monitoring, bias, or who can't access it).
Our group's verdict (the asset classmates most often get wrong + one-sentence justification):
